note:
我们的解释、模式匹配和翻译信息的能力是非凡的，这有助于我们在生活和工作中发挥作用。然而，在进行一场艰难的谈话时，这种过早的分析可能会伤害我们。
例如，如果你的队友问，“有没有办法更快地完成这个项目？”你可能会认为这意味着“你走得太慢了”，或者我不相信你已经想清楚了。
或者，如果你的室友说，“我觉得我最近一直在做所有的家务”，你可能会在内心反对，“那不是真的！”今天早上我洗了碗！“。
一个无害的问题可能会升级为一系列误解，让我们感到受到攻击或被忽视。在大多数情况下，这些反应是潜移默化地发生的，我们会感到不舒服或防御性，而不知道自己是如何做到这一点的。
没有简单的方法来打破(暂停)这一非常自然、非常人性化的解释循环，但如何倾听并达成相互理解的问题是有答案的。这个答案是转述的。
释义就是分享你用自己的话对所听到的内容的解释。
释义会鼓励你注意说话人在说什么，因为你需要把它重复给他们听。
释义可以让说话者在听到镜像回给他们的信息后改正他们自己的信息。
设定意图，在谈话开始时解释说话人所说的话。如果你不能解释他们说的话，请他们重复一遍。
我：我明白了，所以你想让我………。对不起，您能重复一遍吗？
我：你想让我把这个季度的产品路线图寄过来。好的，我会的！
请演讲者重复他们说的话是可以的。你不需要第一次就把它做对，你只需要把它做对就行了。
存在于你头脑中的软件与存在于说话者头脑中的软件不同。给演讲者一个与你合作的机会，以获得正确的解释。
队友：“我注意到你最近在项目会议上没有注意。你介意多参加吗？“。
释义：“你已经注意到我在开会时不专心，而你想让我更多地参与？”
队友：“不，你在大多数会议中都要专心。我只注意到你们在我们每天的项目会议上没有注意到这一点。“
沟通并不容易，有时很难在第一次就做对。给演讲者一个修改他们所说内容的机会。
释义是：“你很难理解我在做什么。我应该给你更多的最新消息吗？“。
经理：“事实上，我知道你在做什么。我只是不能理解这个项目是否还能赶得上最后期限。“。
队友：“当我在解释一些事情时，你打断了我的话，这让我觉得我的意见无关紧要。”
非释义：“我没有意识到我在做这件事，对不起。”
释义：“我已经打断你几次了，这让你觉得我不重视你的意见。我没意识到我在这么做。对不起。“。
迈克尔·施莱纳在“需要被理解”一书中指出，“似乎总是潜伏在背景中的潜意识恐惧是，如果我们不被理解，就好像我们从未存在过一样。”
转述的反应感觉更好，因为它传达了理解，从而证实了我们的存在。当我们感到被理解时，我们就会感到安全。
释义还能让你更容易认出并回应说话者在反馈给你的信息中表达的话语和感受。
队友：“当我在解释一些事情时，你打断了我的话，这让我觉得我的意见无关紧要。”
释义：“我已经打断你几次了，这让你觉得我不重视你的意见。我没意识到我在这么做。我很重视你的意见。对不起。“。
“对劳资谈判的研究表明，如果每个谈判者在回应之前都同意准确地重复前一位发言者所说的话，那么达成冲突解决所需的时间就会减少一半。”-马歇尔·B·罗森伯格(Marshall B.Rosenberg)在“非暴力沟通”(Non Violent Communication)中写道。
释义可以最大限度地减少误解。在谈话结束时，您和演讲者将带着相同的解释离开，这将减少后续工作的需要。
彼得：“嗨，你今天晚上想要TPS报告。你说的TPS是指茶党规格吗？“。
彼得：“你想让我今天晚上把茶会说明书寄给你吗？”
释义在专业环境中很有用，但在个人环境中可能是无价的。
我们越是关心与我们交谈的人，我们就越需要被他们理解，也越害怕被他们看不见。
当与朋友或家人进行情绪激动的谈话时，转述一下，让他们感觉被看到了。
释义：“你感到沮丧、疲惫和悲伤。我是为你而来的。“。
解释一下没有什么坏处，就像在考试中仔细检查答案一样没有坏处，但并不是在每种情况下都能增加价值。
在释义时，请记住您使用的语气很重要。没有一个适用于所有情况的语气，但作为一般的指导方针，使用奇怪的语气而不是陈述性的语气，绝对要避免任何听起来有点讽刺的语气。
你的语气应该传达出澄清和理解演讲者所说的话的愿望。如果你没有这个愿望，不要假装，以后再谈。你可以这样回答，“我需要一点时间来处理这件事。您介意我稍后再联系您继续谈话吗？“。
有时，我会在转述之前先表达我的意图，以防我无法调节语气：“我能把你说的重复一遍吗，以确保我听懂了？”或者“这是我从你说的话中理解的……”
释义就像魔术精灵粉，你可以把它撒在任何谈话上，让它更积极、更有成效。这会让你正在说话的人感到被理解和认可。通过释义，你会节省时间，你的工作会更准确，你会因为善于沟通和倾听而受到称赞。
本周试着转述一下，看看你是否注意到了不同之处。如果这对你来说是一次新的尝试，请回复这篇文章，让我知道进展如何！
感谢您的阅读!。如果你想收到更多关于产生重大影响的小点子的帖子，请订阅下面的帖子！如果你觉得这篇文章很有用，请随时与家人、朋友和同事分享。
note_en:
Our ability to interpret, pattern-match, and translate information is remarkable, and helps us function in life and at work. While engaged in a tough conversation, however, this premature analysis may hurt us.
 For example, if your teammate asks,  “Is there any way this project can be done faster?” you might take that to mean,  “You are moving too slowly,” or  "I don't trust that you've thought this through."
 Or if your roommate says,  “I feel like I’ve been doing all the chores lately,” you might object internally with,  “That’s not true! I did the dishes this morning!”
    A harmless question could escalate into a series of misunderstandings, leaving us feeling attacked or invisible. In most cases, these reactions happen imperceptibly, and we move towards feeling uncomfortable or defensive without knowing how we got there.
 There’s no easy way to  breakpoint (pause) this very natural, very human cycle of interpretation, but there is an answer to the question of how to  listen and come away with mutual understanding. That answer is  paraphrasing.
  Paraphrasing is sharing your interpretation of what you’ve heard in your own words.
  Paraphrasing encourages you to pay attention to what the speaker is saying because you’ll need to repeat it back to them.
  Paraphrasing allows the speaker to correct their own message after hearing it mirrored back to them.
   Set the intention to paraphrase what the speaker says near the beginning of the conversation. If you aren’t able to paraphrase what they said, ask them to repeat it.
   Me: I see, so you want me to………sorry, could you repeat that?
  Me: You want me to send over this quarter’s product roadmap. Yes, will do!
 Asking the speaker to repeat what they said is okay. You don’t need to get it right the first time, you just need to get it right.
  The software that lives inside your mind is not the same as the software that lives inside the speaker’s mind. Give the speaker a chance to work with you to get to the right interpretation.
 Teammate: “I've observed that you haven't been paying attention during the project meetings recently. Would you mind participating more?”
 Paraphrased: “You've noticed I don’t pay attention during meetings, and you want me to participate more?”
 Teammate: “No, you pay attention during most meetings. I’ve only noticed that you haven’t been paying attention during our daily project meetings.”
  Communication isn’t easy, and sometimes it’s hard to get it right the first time. Give the speaker a chance to revise what they said.
  Paraphrased: “You're having trouble understanding what I'm working on. Should I give you more updates?”
 Manager: “Actually, I do know what you’re working on. I’m just unable to understand whether the project will still make the deadline.”
    Teammate: “When you cut me off as I’m explaining something, it makes me feel like my opinion doesn't matter.”
 Non-paraphrased: “I didn’t realize I was doing that, I’m sorry.”
  Paraphrased: “I’ve cut you off a few times, and that makes you feel like I don't value your opinion. I didn’t realize I was doing that. I’m sorry.”
 Michael Schreiner notes in  The Need to be Understood that, “The unconscious fear that seems to always be lurking in the background is that if we aren’t understood it will be as if we never existed.”
 The response with paraphrasing feels better because it conveys understanding and thus validates our existence. When we feel understood, we feel safe.
 Paraphrasing also makes you more likely to recognize and respond to the  words and  feelings the speaker expressed in their feedback to you.
 Teammate: “When you cut me off as I’m explaining something, it makes me feel like my opinion doesn't matter.”
 Paraphrased: “I’ve cut you off a few times, and that makes you feel like I don't value your opinion. I didn’t realize I was doing that.  And I do value your opinion. I’m sorry.”
  “Studies in labor-management negotiations demonstrate that the time required to reach conflict resolution is cut in half when each negotiator agrees, before responding, to accurately repeat what the previous speaker had said.” - Marshall B. Rosenberg in  Nonviolent Communication.
 Paraphrasing minimizes misunderstandings. At the end of a conversation, you and the speaker will leave with the same interpretation, which will reduce the need for a follow-up.
       Peter: “Hi, you wanted the TPS report this evening. By TPS did you mean the tea party spec?”
      Peter: “You want me to send you the tea party spec this evening?”
     Paraphrasing is useful in a professional context, but can be invaluable in a personal context.
 The more we care about the person we’re speaking with, the more we need to feel understood by them and the more we fear becoming invisible to them.
 When engaged in an emotional conversation with friends or family, paraphrase to help them feel seen.
  Paraphrased: “You’re feeling frustrated, tired, and sad. I’m here for you.”
  It never hurts to paraphrase just like it never hurts to double-check your answers on a test, but it doesn’t add value in every situation.
    While paraphrasing, keep in mind that the tone you use is important. There’s no one-size-fits-all tone for every situation, but as a general guideline, use a curious tone over a declarative tone, and definitely steer clear of any tone that sounds remotely sarcastic.
   Your tone should convey the desire to clarify and understand what the speaker said. If you don't have this desire, don't fake it, just revisit the conversation later. You can respond with something like,  “I need a moment to process this. Would you mind if I reached out to you later to continue the conversation?”
 Sometimes, I express my intent upfront before paraphrasing, just in case I’m unable to regulate my tone:  “Can I repeat what you said back to you, just to make sure I understand?” or “ Here’s what I understood from what you said...”
  Paraphrasing is like magic pixie dust you can sprinkle onto any conversation to make it more positive and productive. It will leave the person you're speaking with feeling understood and validated. By paraphrasing, you will save time, your work will be more accurate, and you'll be applauded for being a good communicator and listener.
 Try paraphrasing this week and see if you notice a difference. If this is a new experiment for you, please respond to this post and let me know how it went!
  Thank you for reading! If you’d like to receive more posts about small ideas that produce a big impact, please subscribe below! If you found this post useful, feel free to share it with family, friends, and coworkers.
title: 不再有误解：解释-何时、为什么和如何
title_en: No More Misunderstandings: Paraphrasing – When, Why, and How

FinCEN文件揭示了全球银行在工业规模洗钱中的角色-以及随之而来的流血和痛苦。
这项全球调查利用了一批秘密金融情报报告，揭示了银行的利润动机如何压倒了它们阻止脏钱的法律义务-以及以美国为首的崩溃的执法系统如何让业务照常进行。
国际调查记者联合会(International Consortium Of Investigative Journers)的一项数据分析发现，银行经常在不知道资金最终来源或目的地的情况下处理交易，在与洗钱和腐败有潜在联系的交易中，往往是与秘密司法管辖区成立的空壳公司之间的往来。分析还发现，从可疑交易的时间到银行提交报告的时间存在滞后。
泄露的文件被称为FinCEN Files，其中包括银行和其他金融公司向美国财政部金融犯罪执法网络提交的2100多份可疑活动报告，即SARS。该机构简称为FinCEN，是全球打击洗钱系统的核心情报单位。
全球合作探索了1999-2017年间超过2万亿美元的交易，这些交易在近90家金融机构的2100多份报告中被标记出来。FinCEN文件中的大多数SARS-98%-是在2011-2017年间提交的。FinCEN文件还包含交易电子表格和FinCEN报告，使总缓存达到约2600个文档。
在金融机构在2011年至2017年提交的1200多万份可疑活动报告中，FinCEN文件只占不到0.02%。
据BuzzFeed News报道，其中一些记录是作为美国国会对俄罗斯干预2016年美国总统选举的调查的一部分收集的；其他记录是在执法机构向FinCEN提出要求后收集的。BuzzFeed News获得了这些记录，并与ICIJ和来自88个国家108家新闻机构的记者分享，以此作为为期16个月的洗钱和名牌银行角色调查的基础。
FinCEN文件中的可疑活动报告是一堆杂乱无章的文件，反映了全球银行洗钱合规官员的私下担忧。SARS包括一段叙述，以及附加的电子表格，其中有时有数百行原始交易数据。这些报告的质量各不相同：一些报告非常详细，描述了银行所说的具有洗钱所有特征的交易。其他人则缺少重要信息，反映出银行本身对它们为高风险客户和其他金融机构转移的数十亿美元缺乏洞察力。有些记录只是简单的电子表格，填满了交易方名称、银行名称、数字和日期，在FinCEN文件中，这些记录与说明无关，这将提供包含它们的原因。
例如，在FinCEN文件中，合规性官员有时会将主要地址的空格留空。超过五分之一的报告的地址字段包括至少一个或多个标记的主题-包括个人和空壳公司-没有街道号码、城市，甚至没有国家，这些应该用两个字符的代码指定。在某些情况下，空白地址是银行自己的公司网络中的客户。
ICIJ发现，当地址被包括在内时，超过一半的FinCEN Files SARS列出了错误的国家代码。例如，在中国的地址有时会被分配瑞士的“CH”国家代码。
2018年财政部监察长的一份报告发现，“申请者报告某些关键数据字段的方式不一致，如机构名称或地址。”该报告对2013年5月至2014年4月提交的175万多份SARS和相关文件中的39个关键数据字段进行审查，发现一个或多个“数据质量错误”，包括遗漏地址和其他关键数据字段留空--33.5%的文件中有这些错误，报告还表示，“没有机制”来确保SAR中的错误得到纠正。
作为回应，该机构的管理层表示，它已经进行了改革，认为这些改革“在数据质量与数据紧迫性和有用性之间取得了适当的平衡”。
挖掘数据和探索资金流是一个项目中的项目。ICIJ协调了一项涉及30个国家的85多名记者的大规模全球努力，从包含特区叙事报告的PDF文件中提取数据，并收集了17,600多条额外记录，其中许多记录是通过信息自由请求收集的。
ICIJ在其定制的共享和研究平台Datashare上与合作伙伴共享这些记录，该平台由ICIJ的技术团队开发。
ICIJ及其合作伙伴使用统计和文本分析对数据进行了分析。ICIJ还建立了一个定制的事实核查工具来处理提取的数据，并部署了机器学习来审查作为数据一部分的6万多个地址。后来手动检查了所有地址。
FinCEN文件缓存中的大多数SAR叙述不包括包含事务级数据的附加电子表格。但由于叙述往往包含有关资金流的关键细节，ICIJ、BuzzFeed News和媒体合作伙伴探索了这些报告的大约300万字作为分析的一部分。
ICIJ的分析发现，在一半的报告中，银行没有交易背后一个或多个实体的信息。在FinCEN档案中的680多份报告中，金融机构要求提供更多有关实体的信息，其他银行超过160次没有回应。瑞士等国的一些银行或分行援引其管辖范围内的当地保密法否认这些信息。
ICIJ的一项分析还发现，FinCEN Files中的银行定期为在所谓的保密司法管辖区注册的公司处理交易，而且这样做时不知道账户的最终所有者。在620多份报告中，银行至少一次指出使用了“高风险”司法管辖区。公司账户持有人经常提供英国、美国、塞浦路斯、香港、阿联酋、俄罗斯和瑞士的地址。至少有20%的报告包含一名客户，其地址位于世界顶级离岸金融避风港之一--英属维尔京群岛。
德意志银行提交的982份文件占此次泄密事件中可疑交易总金额的62%。FinCEN文件还包含来自纽约梅隆银行(Bank Of New York Mellon)、渣打银行(Standard Chartered)、摩根大通(JP Morgan Chase)、巴克莱(Barclays)和汇丰银行(HSBC)的大量文件。
ICIJ的分析显示，从交易发生到向FinCEN报告的时间，滞后时间的中位数为166天-几乎是半年。联邦法规要求金融机构在大多数情况下在发现可疑交易后30天内上报。
分析发现，在一些案例中，银行为回应新闻报道而提交报告，(包括ICIJ 2016年巴拿马文件调查)，或者在交易发生很久之后才提交涉及客户的司法法律文件。
ICIJ还发现了与银行标记的20多家公司和个人有关的可疑交易，这些公司和个人与腐败、欺诈、挪用公款或逃避制裁案件有关(并制作了互动节目，展示这些客户的关键细节)。
分析发现，涉嫌洗钱活动是在FinCEN Files中提交报告的最常见原因。其他原因是涉嫌欺诈，这是FinCEN的一个类别，称为“金融工具(货币合约)”，以及涉嫌所谓的结构性交易，这是一系列旨在避免危险信号的交易。
在删除重复项、标准化银行名称和其他初步步骤后，ICIJ进行了文本分析，以找出叙述中可能表明存在空壳公司或银行不知道最终所有者的句子。ICIJ使用SQL和Python编程语言进行分析。
ICIJ、BuzzFeed News及其合作伙伴尝试了多种编程形式，试图自动从8000多页的叙述中提取细节(大部分仍是手工完成的)。首先，ICIJ合作伙伴SVT使用机器学习来筛选记录并获得第一组交易数据。语言上的差异和报告的复杂性阻碍了对一些关键细节的捕捉。
最后，ICIJ及其合作伙伴发起了一项巨大的数据提取工作：在一年多的时间里，来自30个国家的85名记者从指定的可疑活动报告中审查和提取交易信息，并手动将其输入Excel文件，然后上传到ICIJ的通信平台Global iHub。这项努力产生了5.5万条结构化数据记录，并包括SARS期间银行标记的20多万笔交易的细节。
提取完成后，ICIJ对每个提取进行了三次审查。仅事实核查就花了七个月的时间。利用Django网络框架，ICIJ建立了自己的事实核查工具，突出每个记者提取的信息，允许同事在整个过程中标记错误并跟踪编辑。
ICIJ为合作伙伴举办了广泛的培训课程，介绍如何使用ICIJ的技术进行研究，并举行后续审查会议，以更好地理解数据。该项目还广泛利用ICIJ的Global iHub和安全电话会议来协调这项复杂的工作。
通过这一巨大努力，ICIJ得以找到FinCEN文件中原本会隐藏的3806亿美元以上的细节，例如，包括报告的涉及黄金交易公司Kaloti的93亿美元以上的可疑交易。在FinCEN Files调查中审查的可疑交易总额中，超过四分之一与黄金有关。
这一巨大的提取努力帮助跟踪了代理银行，即可以进入美联储的全球银行，由他们在世界各地的金融机构客户提供。分析发现，在FinCEN Files司法管辖区，拉脱维亚和香港是当地银行通过代理银行收款或汇款的最常见地点。
ICIJ还发现，所谓的有限责任合伙企业在英国政府财务报表中提交的金额，与银行合规官员报告的流经这些公司账户的金额之间存在巨大差异。ICIJ发现，在FinCEN文件中，超过45亿美元作为公司之家(Companies House)的收入在财务报表中流经有限责任公司的账户，而不是有限责任公司的报告。公司之家是英国的公司注册处，长期以来一直因允许公司向秘密所有者注册而受到批评。
ICIJ还使用委内瑞拉承包商登记处和公共记录数据库Sayari和Vendata的信息，在FinCEN文件中确定了2009年至2017年期间报告的与委内瑞拉有联系的可疑交易超过48亿美元。其中近70%列出了委内瑞拉政府实体，如财政部，作为交易的一方。
最后，ICIJ使用图形数据库(Neo4J和Linkurary)来可视化和浏览FinCEN Files的400个电子表格，其中包含100,000个交易的数据。这些都是帮助拼凑出一个支离破碎的系统的微妙图景的众多工具中的一部分。
全球数据协作撰稿人：Kenzi Abou-Sabe，Krishna Acharya，Rickard Andersson，Roman Anin，Agustin Armendariz，Peter Babuzky，Jeremie Baruch，Helena Bengtsson，Paolo Biondani，Jacob Borg，Lars Bové，Simon Bowers，Andrea Cardenas，Claire Caruana，Miriam Castillo，Daniela Castro，Sasha Chavkin，Mario Christodoulou，Kristof Clerix，Anthony Irzbon，Jelena Cosic，Xavier Counasse，Gaby de Groot，Xavier Díaz-Strike，Lara Dimis，Dolina Dolinina，Philipp Eckstein，Marcus Engert，Jeses Escudero，Migandor Gutierrez，Kaspar Finstro，Mario Christodoulou，Kristof Clerix，Anthony Irzbon，Jelena Cosic，Xavier Counasse，Gaby de Groot，Philipp Eckstein，Marcus Engert，Marcus Escudero，Migandor Gutierrez。Karlijn Kuijpers，Ville Juutilainen，Karrie Kehoe，Jiyoon Kim，Minna Knus-Galán，Anouk Kootstra，Bernt Koscheh，Ulla Kramar-Schmid，Tomohiro Kubota，Andrew Lehren，BoYoung Lim，Lee Long Hue，Norihisa Makino，Alesya Marohovskaya，Stefan Melichar，Carmen Molina Acosta，Gretchen Morgenson，Michael Nikbakhsh，Toshihiro Okuyama，Marco Oved，Andras Petho，Scott Pham，Petra Pichler，Kit Ramgopal，Aidila Razak，Delphine Ruter，Michael，Saluomi Sawa，Nina Selbo Set，Karina Shefhsh，Shley nov，Emily，Siegel，Freemy Vicher，Freemy Vichler，Kit Ramgopal，Aidila Razak，Delphine Ruter，Michael，Saluomi Sawa，Nina Selbo，Karina Shefrofsky，Shley nov，Olesya Shmagun，Siegel，Freemy Vicher，Delphine Ruster，Michael，Saluomi Sawa，Nina Selbo。Mika Velikovski，Tom Warren，Marget Williams，Amy Wilson-Chapman，Spencer Woodman，Shyamlal Yadav，Blanka Zoldi
note_en:
The  FinCEN Files reveals the role of global banks in industrial-scale money laundering – and the bloodshed and suffering that flow in its wake.
 Drawing on a cache of secret financial intelligence reports, the global investigation reveals how  banks’ profit motives overwhelm their legal obligations to stop dirty money — and how a broken U.S.-led enforcement system perpetuates business as usual.
 A data analysis by the  International Consortium of Investigative Journalists found banks routinely processed transactions without knowing the ultimate source or destination of the money, often to and from shell companies incorporated in secrecy jurisdictions in transactions with potential links to money laundering and corruption. The analysis also found lags from the time of a suspicious transaction to banks’ filing a report.
 The leaked documents, known as the FinCEN Files, include more than 2,100  suspicious activity reports, or SARs, filed by banks and other financial firms with the U.S. Department of Treasury’s Financial Crimes Enforcement Network. The agency, known in shorthand as FinCEN, is an intelligence unit at the heart of the global system to fight money laundering.
 The global collaboration explored more than $2 trillion transactions dated from 1999-2017 that had been flagged in the more than 2,100 reports by nearly 90 financial institutions. Most of the SARs in the FinCEN Files – 98% – were filed from 2011-2017. The FinCEN Files also contain transaction spreadsheets and FinCEN reports, bringing the total cache to about 2,600 documents.
 The FinCEN Files represent less than 0.02% of the more than 12 million suspicious activity reports that financial institutions filed between 2011 and 2017.
 According to  BuzzFeed News, some of the records were gathered as part of U.S. congressional investigations into Russian interference in the 2016 U.S. presidential election;  others were gathered following requests to FinCEN from law enforcement agencies. BuzzFeed News obtained the records and shared them with ICIJ, and journalists from 108 news organizations in 88 countries,  to use as a basis of a 16-month investigation into money laundering and the role played by name-brand banks.
  The suspicious activity reports in the FinCEN Files are a sprawling jumble of documents that reflect the private concerns of global bank money-laundering compliance officers. The SARs include a narrative along with attached spreadsheets of sometimes hundreds of lines of raw transaction data. The reports are of varying quality: some are highly detailed, describing transactions that banks say bear all the hallmarks of money laundering. Others are missing vital information, and reflect a lack of insight by banks themselves about the billions of dollars they are moving for high-risk clients and for other financial institutions. Some records are simply spreadsheets filled with party names, bank names, figures, and dates, that in the FinCEN Files came unattached to the narrative that would provide a reason for their inclusion.
 For instance, in the FinCEN Files, compliance officers sometimes leave blank the space intended for the primary address. The address field for more than a fifth of the reports include at least one or more of the flagged subjects – both individuals and shell companies – with no street number, city, or even country, which are supposed to be designated with a two-character code. In some cases, the blank addresses are for customers in the bank’s own corporate network.
 And when an address was included, more than half of the FinCEN Files SARs listed the wrong country code, ICIJ found. For example, on occasions an address in China would have Switzerland’s “CH” country code assigned to it.
 A 2018 Treasury Department Inspector General  report found “inconsistencies in how filers report certain critical data fields such as institution name or address.” The report’s review of 39 critical data fields in more than 1.75 million SARs and related documents filed from May 2013 – April 2014, found one or more “data quality errors”  including omitting addresses and other critical data fields left blank –  in 33.5% percent of the filings.”  The report also said there was “no mechanism in place” to ensure that errors in SAR were corrected.
 In response, the agency’s management said it had made reforms that it believed “strike the proper balance of data quality with data urgency and usefulness.”
 Mining the data and exploring the money flows was a project-within-a-project. ICIJ coordinated a massive global effort involving more than 85 journalists in 30 countries to extract data from the PDF files that contained the SAR narrative reports, as well as to gather more than 17,600 additional records, many via freedom of information requests.
 ICIJ shared the records with partners on its bespoke sharing and research platform,  Datashare, which is developed by ICIJ’s technical team.
 ICIJ and its partners analyzed the data using statistical and textual analysis. ICIJ also built a bespoke fact-checking tool to process the extracted data and deployed machine learning to review more than 60,000 addresses that were part of the data. All addresses were later checked manually.
  Most of the SAR narratives in the FinCEN Files cache didn’t include attached spreadsheets containing transaction-level data. But since the narratives often contained key details about money flows, ICIJ, BuzzFeed News and media partners explored the reports’ roughly 3 million words as part of the analysis.
   ICIJ’s analysis found that in half of the reports, banks didn’t have information about one or more entities behind the transactions. In more than 680 reports in the FinCEN Files, financial institutions asked for more information about entities and on more than 160 occasions other banks didn’t respond. Some banks or branches in countries such as Switzerland cited local secrecy laws in their jurisdictions to deny the information.
 An ICIJ analysis also found that banks in the FinCEN Files regularly processed transactions for companies registered in so-called secrecy jurisdictions and did so without knowing the ultimate owner of the account. In more than 620 of the reports, banks flagged the use of “high risk” jurisdictions at least once. Corporate account holders often provided addresses in the U.K., the U.S., Cyprus, Hong Kong, the United Arab Emirates, Russia and Switzerland. At least 20% of the reports contained a client with an address in one of the world’s top offshore financial havens, the British Virgin Islands.
 Deutsche Bank’s 982 filings represented 62% of the total amount in suspicious transactions in the leak. The FinCEN Files also contain large numbers of files from Bank of New York Mellon, Standard Chartered, JP Morgan Chase, Barclays and HSBC.
  ICIJ’s analysis revealed a median time lag of 166 days -almost half a year- from the time transactions took place and the time it was reported to FinCEN. Federal rules require financial institutions to report a suspicious transaction in most cases within 30 days after detecting it.
 The analysis found some cases in which banks file reports in response to news reports, (including ICIJ’s 2016  Panama Papers investigation), or judicial legal filings involving customers long after the fact of the transactions.
 ICIJ also found suspicious transactions tied to more than  20 companies and individuals flagged by the banks that were linked to corruption, fraud, embezzlement or sanctions evasion cases (and produced an interactive to present key details about these clients).
 The analysis found that suspicion of money laundering operations was the most common reason given for filing a report in the FinCEN Files. Other reasons were suspicion of fraud, a FinCEN category called “financial instruments (monetary contracts),” and suspicion of so-called structuring, a series of transactions designed to avoid red flags.
  After removing duplicates, standardizing bank names and other preliminary steps, ICIJ performed textual analysis to identify sentences in the narratives that could indicate the presence of a shell company or that a bank didn’t know the ultimate owner. ICIJ used the SQL and Python programming languages for the analysis.
 ICIJ, BuzzFeed News and its partners tried more than one form of programming in an attempt to extract details from the more than 8,000 pages of narratives automatically (much was still done by hand). At first, ICIJ partner SVT used machine learning to screen the records and obtain a first set of transactional data. The variations in language and the complexity of the reports prevented the capture of some key details.
 In the end, ICIJ and its partners launched a giant data-extraction effort: for more than a year, 85 journalists in 30 countries reviewed and extracted transaction information from assigned suspicious activity reports and manually entered it into Excel files, which were then uploaded to ICIJ’s communications platform, the Global iHub. The effort resulted in 55,000 records of structured data and included details on more than 200,000 transactions flagged by the banks in the SARs.
 After the extraction was complete, ICIJ reviewed each extraction three times. The fact-checking alone took seven months. Using the Django web framework, ICIJ built its own fact-checking tool that highlighted the information extracted by each reporter, allowing colleagues to flag errors and track edits throughout the process.
  ICIJ held extensive training sessions for partners on the use of ICIJ’s technologies for research and follow up review sessions to better understand the data. The project also made extensive use of ICIJ’s Global iHub and secure conference calls to coordinate the complex undertaking.
 Through this massive effort, ICIJ was able to find details that would have otherwise remained hidden on more than $380.6 billion in the FinCEN Files, including, for instance, more than $9.3 billion in reported suspicious transactions involving the gold trading company Kaloti. More than a fourth of the total amount of suspicious transactions reviewed as part of the FinCEN Files investigation were related to gold.
 This giant extraction effort helped track correspondent banks, global banks with access to the U.S. Federal Reserve, by their financial institution customers around the world. The analysis found that in the FinCEN Files jurisdictions as Latvia and Hong Kong were among the most common locations of local banks receiving or sending money via correspondent banks.
   ICIJ also found huge discrepancies between the amount that so-called limited liability partnerships had filed in UK government financial statements and the amount bank compliance officers reported flowing through the same companies’ accounts. ICIJ found more than $4.5 billion appeared in the FinCEN Files as flowing through LLP accounts more than the LLPs report in their financial statements as revenue to Companies House, the registrar of companies in the United Kingdom long criticized for allowing corporations to register with secret owners.
 ICIJ also used information from the Venezuelan Registry of Contractors and public records databases Sayari and Vendata to identify in the FinCEN Files more than $4.8 billion in reported suspicious transactions with links to Venezuela between 2009 and 2017. Nearly 70% of that amount listed a Venezuelan government entity, such as the Ministry of Finance, as a party to the transaction.
  Finally, ICIJ used graph databases ( Neo4J and  Linkurious) to visualize and explore the FinCEN Files’ 400 spreadsheets containing data on 100,000 transactions. These were among the many tools to help piece together a nuanced picture of a broken system.
 Global data collaboration contributors: Kenzi Abou-Sabe, Krishna Acharya, Rickard Andersson, Roman Anin, Agustin Armendariz, Peter Babutzky, Jeremie Baruch, Helena Bengtsson, Paolo Biondani, Jacob Borg, Lars Bové, Simon Bowers, Andrea Cardenas, Claire Caruana, Miriam Castillo, Daniela Castro, Sasha Chavkin, Mario Christodoulou, Kristof Clerix, Anthony Cormier, Jelena Cosic, Xavier Counasse, Gaby De Groot, Emilia Díaz-Struck, Lara Dihmis, Irina Dolinina, Philipp Eckstein, Marcus Engert, Jesus Escudero, Miguel Fiandor Gutierrez, Kaspar Fink, Mariel Fitz Patrick, Will Fitzgibbon, Azeen Ghorayshi, Kyra Gurney, Günter Hack, John Hansen, Richard Holmes, Karol Ilagan, Maia Jastreblansky, Karlijn Kuijpers, Ville Juutilainen, Karrie Kehoe, Jiyoon Kim, Minna Knus-Galán, Anouk Kootstra, Bernt Koschuh, Ulla Kramar-Schmid, Tomohiro Kubota, Andrew Lehren, Boyoung Lim, Lee Long Hui, Norihisa Makino, Alesya Marohovskaya, Stefan Melichar, Carmen Molina Acosta, Gretchen Morgenson, Michael Nikbakhsh, Toshihiro Okuyama, Marco Oved, Andras Petho, Scott Pham, Petra Pichler, Kit Ramgopal, Aidila Razak, Delphine Reuter, Michael Sallah, Yasuomi Sawa, Nina Selbo Torset, Karina Shedrofsky, Roman Shleynov, Olesya Shmagun, Emily Siegel, Jeremy Singer-Vine, Fredrik Stalnacke, Jan Strozyk, John Templon, Rory Tinman, Mago Torres, Yukiko Toyoda, Maxime Vaudano, Mika Velikovskiy, Tom Warren, Margot Williams, Amy Wilson-Chapman, Spencer Woodman, Shyamlal Yadav, Blanka Zoldi
title: 从杂乱无章的秘密报告，关于大银行和黑钱的该死的数据中脱颖而出
title_en: From a jumble of secret reports, damning data on big banks and dirty money

note:
这项任务是对十几种不同的低温流体管理系统的测试，其中包括液氢，液氢确实是一种很难处理的物质。测试将在轨道上的单一航天器上进行，这意味着它的设计和适应将特别复杂。
当然，有效载荷本身及其低温系统将由洛克希德公司和他们在NASA的合作伙伴设计和建造，但该公司在开发过程中需要与发射供应商密切合作，特别是在实际发射之前。
相对论创始人兼首席执行官蒂姆·埃利斯解释说，该公司从上到下3D打印整个火箭的方法特别适合这一点。
“我们正在建造一个定制的有效载荷整流罩，它有他们需要的特定有效载荷加载接口，定制配件和适配器，”他说。“当然，它仍然需要平滑--对于一个外行来说，它看起来就像是一枚普通的火箭，”他补充道。
每个整流罩(运载火箭覆盖有效载荷的外部部分)都必须是定制的，但这一个更是如此。在发射前对十几个低温操作进行加载和测试，这一微妙之处需要进行大量修改，而在其他日子里，这些修改将导致制造复杂性的大幅增加。
埃利斯解释说：“如果你看看今天使用的制造工具，你会发现它们与过去60年没有太大不同。”“它是固定的工具，巨大的机器，看起来令人印象深刻，但只制作了一个形状或一个手工设计的物体。这需要12-24个月的时间才能完成。“。
埃利斯说：“使用我们的3D打印方法，我们可以在30天内打印出整个整流罩。”“它也是软件定义的，所以我们只需更改文件即可更改尺寸和形状。对于这个特定的对象，我们有一些自定义功能，我们可以更快地完成这些功能并进行调整。尽管任务还有三年的时间，但随着发射的临近，总会有最后一刻的变化，我们可以适应这一点。否则，你现在就得锁定设计了。“。
埃利斯对有机会与这样一个主要承包商公开承担任务感到兴奋。这些庞大的公司投入了数十亿美元的政府资金，并参与了许多启动活动，因此，重要的是要在他们的好账簿上，或者至少在他们的名单上。像这样一个复杂但风险相对较低的任务(与载人发射或价值数十亿美元的卫星相比)，对于像相对论这样的公司来说，是展示其能力的绝佳机会。(由于已经预售了许多运载火箭，人们对3D打印运载火箭显然不乏兴趣，但总是越多越好。)
不过，如果一切继续按计划进行，该公司将在那之前进入太空。首次轨道试飞定于2021年底进行。埃利斯提到：“实际上，我们现在正在打印发布硬件，最近几周。”
美国国家航空航天局(NASA)的引爆点项目为洛克希德公司提供了8970万美元的这项实验合同，顾名思义，该项目旨在帮助将前景看好的技术转化为商业可行性。由于每年有数亿美元奖励给从事登月器和机械臂等项目的公司，这有点像该机构的风险基金。
note_en:
The mission is a test of a dozen different cryogenic fluid management systems, including liquid hydrogen, which is a very difficult substance to work with indeed. The tests will take place on a single craft in orbit, which means it will be a particularly complicated one to design and accommodate.
 The payload itself and its cryogenic systems will be designed and built by Lockheed and their partners at  NASA,   of course, but the company will need to work closely with its launch provider during development and especially in the leadup to the actual launch.
  Relativity founder and CEO Tim Ellis explained that the company’s approach of 3D printing the entire rocket top to bottom is especially well suited for this.
  “We’re building a custom payload fairing that has specific payload loading interfaces they need, custom fittings and adapters,” he said. “It still needs to be smooth, of course — to a lay person it will look like a normal rocket,” he added.
 Every fairing (the external part of the launch vehicle covering the payload) is necessarily custom, but this one much more so. The delicacy of having a dozen cryogenic operations being loaded up and tested until moments before launch necessitates a number of modifications that, in other days, would result in a massive increase in manufacturing complexity.
 “If you look at the manufacturing tools being used today, they’re not much different from the last 60 years,” Ellis explained. “It’s fixed tooling, giant machines that look impressive but only make one shape or one object that’s been designed by hand. And it’ll take 12-24 months to make it.”
   “With our 3D printed approach we can print the entire fairing in under 30 days,” Ellis said. “It’s also software defined, so we can just change the file to change the dimensions and shape. For this particular object we have some custom features that we’re able to do more quickly and adapt. Even though the mission is three years out, there will always be last minute changes as you get closer to launch, and we can accommodate that. Otherwise you’d have to lock in the design now.”
 Ellis was excited about the opportunity to publicly take on a mission with such a major contractor. These enormous companies field billions of government dollars and take part in many launches, so it’s important to be in their good books, or at least in their rolodexes. A mission like this, complex but comparatively low stakes (compared with a crewed launch or billion-dollar satellite) is a great chance for a company like Relativity to show its capabilities. (Having presold many of its launches already, there’s clearly no lack of interest in the 3D printed launch vehicles, but more is always better.)
  The company will be going to space before then, though, if all continues to go according to plan. The first orbital test flight is scheduled for late 2021. “We’re actually printing the launch hardware right now, the last few weeks,” Ellis mentioned.
 The  NASA Tipping Point program that is funding Lockheed with an $89.7 million contract for this experiment is one intended to, as its name indicates, help tip promising technologies over the edge into commercial viability. With  hundreds of millions awarded yearly for companies pursuing things like lunar hoppers and robotic arms, it’s a bit like the agency’s venture fund.
title: 洛克希德公司为NASA实验任务挑选相对论的3D打印火箭
title_en: Lockheed picks Relativity’s 3D-printed rocket for experimental NASA mission
